content.full_text:
The Resources Agency

Pete Wilson                                                Douglas P. Wheeler
Governor                                                Secretary '92

of California

California Conservation Corps • Department of Boating & Waterways • Department of Conservation
Department of Fish & Game • Department of Forestry & Fire Protection • Department of Parks & Recreation • Department of Water Resources


April 17, 1992


John Wallace
Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

RE: Advise Letter regarding potential conflict of interest for
Coastal Commissioner Steve MacElvaine.

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Pursuant to our conversations during the last week, I am writing
to request an Advise Letter from the FPPC regarding a potential
conflict of interest for Coastal Commissioner Steve MacElvaine.
I would like to request this letter by May 11.

FACTS

Steve MacElvaine is and has been a member of the California
Coastal Commission since 1983.  Chevron regularly has permits and
other matters before the California Coastal Commission.  Mr.
MacElvaine has recused himself from those deliberations,
concerned that he may have a conflict of interest because he
leases some grazing land from Chevron near its Estero terminal in
Morro Bay.

For more than 20 years, Mr. MacElvaine has grazed cattle on
property he leases from Chevron.  According to the terms of the
lease currently in effect, Mr. MacElvaine pays Chevron $842.80
per year to lease approximately 341 acres of land in the Morro
Bay area for cattle grazing.  The Company may terminate the lease
at any time upon 30 days written notice to Mr. MacElvaine.

The property leased to Mr. MacElvaine is a portion of a tract of
land maintained by Chevron as a buffer zone for one of its
terminals.  Chevron leases other portions of the same buffer

The Resources Building  Sacramento, CA 95814  (916) 653-5656  FAX (916) 653-8102

California Coastal Commission • California Tahoe Conservancy • Colorado River Board of California
Energy Resources, Conservation & Development Commission • San Francisco Bay Conservation & Development Commission
State Coastal Conservancy • State Lands Commission • State Reclamation Board

Printed on recycled paper

property to three other tenants for cattle grazing. The terms of
Mr. MacElvaine's lease are substantially similar to the terms of
the other three grazing leases. MacElvaine, 341 acres at $2.47
per acre, Domingos Silvera, 120 acres at $2.08 per acre, Peter
Negranti, 837 acres at $2.94 per acre, and James Negranti, 543
acres at $3.31 per acre. All leases represent an approximate
fair market value for grazing in the area.

Given these facts, does Mr. MacElvaine, as a member of the
California Coastal Commission and one who holds a grazing lease
with Chevron, have a conflict of interest with matters that come
before the Coastal Commission which involve Chevron?

I have attached a brief analysis done by counsel representing
Chevron which articulates their opinion.

Because the Coastal Commission will consider a matter involving
Chevron at its May 13, 1992 meeting, I would greatly appreciate
it if you could transmit the advise letter to me not later than
May 11, 1992.

Thank you for your cooperation and please feel free to call on me
if you require additional information.

Sincerely,

Will Shafroth
Assistant Secretary
for Land and Coastal Resources

Attachment

cc: Steve MacElvaine

B.  Commissioner Steve MacElvaine.

For more than 20 years, Mr. MacElvaine has grazed cattle on property leased from the Company.  According to the terms of the lease currently in effect, Mr. MacElvaine pays the Company $642.80 per year to lease approximately 100 acres of land in Morro Bay for cattle grazing.  The Company may terminate the lease at any time upon 30 days' written notice to Mr. MacElvaine.

The property leased to Mr. MacElvaine is a portion of a tract of land maintained by the Company as a buffer zone for one of its terminals.  The Company leases other portions of the same buffer property to three other tenants for cattle grazing.  The terms of Mr. MacElvaine's lease are substantially similar to the terms of the other three grazing leases.

Based on these facts, we assume that Mr. MacElvaine is paying fair rental value to the Company for his grazing lease, and thus he does not have an economic interest in the Company as a result of such lease.  See Datioff Advice Letter, FPPC No. I-91-218 (May 24, 1991); James Advice Letter, FPPC No. I-89-232 (May 17, 1989), copies attached.  The lease agreement does not provide Mr. MacElvaine with an "investment" in the Company (Gov't Code §§ 82034, 87103(a)); the Company is not a source of income to Mr. MacElvaine (Gov't Code §§ 82030, 87103(o)); Mr. MacElvaine does not hold any business position in the Company (Gov't Code § 87103(d)), and because Mr. MacElvaine pays the fair market value in rent, he has not received any gift from the Company (Gov't Code §§ 82028, 87103(e)).  Finally, for purposes of the Act, Mr. MacElvaine may have an interest in real property because of his leasehold interest; however, he still would not have any interest in the Company (Gov't Code §§ 82033, 87103(b); see 2 Cal.Code Regs. § 18233, copy attached.

Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the lease does not create any economic interest for Mr. MacElvaine which would disqualify him from participating in governmental decisions that would have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the Company.

sections.facts:
Steve MacElvaine is and has been a member of the California Coastal Commission since 1983. Chevron regularly has permits and other matters before the California Coastal Commission. Mr. MacElvaine has recused himself from those deliberations, concerned that he may have a conflict of interest because he leases some grazing land from Chevron near its Estero terminal in Morro Bay.

For more than 20 years, Mr. MacElvaine has grazed cattle on property he leases from Chevron. According to the terms of the lease currently in effect, Mr. MacElvaine pays Chevron $842.80 per year to lease approximately 341 acres of land in the Morro Bay area for cattle grazing. The Company may terminate the lease at any time upon 30 days written notice to Mr. MacElvaine.

The property leased to Mr. MacElvaine is a portion of a tract of land maintained by Chevron as a buffer zone for one of its terminals. Chevron leases other portions of the same buffer

property to three other tenants for cattle grazing. The terms of Mr. MacElvaine's lease are substantially similar to the terms of the other three grazing leases. MacElvaine, 341 acres at $2.47 per acre, Domingos Silvera, 120 acres at $2.08 per acre, Peter Negranti, 837 acres at $2.94 per acre, and James Negranti, 543 acres at $3.31 per acre. All leases represent an approximate fair market value for grazing in the area.

Given these facts, does Mr. MacElvaine, as a member of the California Coastal Commission and one who holds a grazing lease with Chevron, have a conflict of interest with matters that come before the Coastal Commission which involve Chevron?

I have attached a brief analysis done by counsel representing Chevron which articulates their opinion.

Because the Coastal Commission will consider a matter involving Chevron at its May 13, 1992 meeting, I would greatly appreciate it if you could transmit the advise letter to me not later than May 11, 1992.

Thank you for your cooperation and please feel free to call on me if you require additional information.
